title: CB4A — Credential Broker for Agents
description: Model B (Token Minting): An AI agent obtains a short-lived, DPoP-bound access token via the Credential Broker for Agents protocol. A Policy Decision Point (PDP) and Credential Delivery Point (CDP) collaborate so that neither holds unilateral power — the PDP never touches credentials, and the CDP never makes policy decisions.

steps.label: GET /workload-api/svid

artifacts.raw_input: Node Attestation: TPM/k8s-sat → Workload Attestation: process UID + cgroup → SVID issued

steps.label: 1. Node Attestation
steps.value: SPIRE Agent on the host proves its identity to the SPIRE Server using a node-specific attestor (e.g., Kubernetes Service Account Token, TPM, or cloud IAM). The SPIRE Server verifies and registers the node.

steps.label: 2. Workload Attestation
steps.value: When the workload (AI Agent process) calls the Workload API, the SPIRE Agent inspects OS kernel attributes (process UID, cgroup membership, k8s pod labels) to confirm the caller's identity matches a registered entry.

steps.label: 3. SVID Issuance
steps.value: SPIRE Server mints an X.509-SVID: a short-lived X.509 certificate with a SubjectAlternativeName of type URI set to the SPIFFE ID. The private key is generated by the SPIRE Agent and never leaves the node.

steps.label: 4. Resulting SPIFFE ID
steps.value:
spiffe://example.org/agent/task-runner-7f9b2

This URI unambiguously identifies the workload across all SPIFFE-aware infrastructure.

steps.explanation: The AI Agent bootstraps its cryptographic identity by calling the SPIFFE Workload API served by a local SPIRE Agent (over a Unix domain socket). SPIRE verifies the workload's OS-level attributes and issues a short-lived X.509 SVID — a certificate whose Subject Alternative Name encodes the SPIFFE ID. This SVID is the agent's proof-of-identity for all subsequent CB4A interactions.
annotations: The private key for the SVID is generated on-node by the SPIRE Agent and never transmitted anywhere — only the certificate (public key + SPIFFE ID) is returned.
SVIDs have short TTLs (typically 1 hour) and are automatically rotated by the SPIRE Agent before expiry.
The Workload API is exposed over a Unix domain socket, limiting access to processes on the same host — an additional OS-level access control layer.

steps.label: POST /credential-request
2. Sign Envelope with SVID Private Key
steps.value:
RS256(
  Base64Url(header) + "." + Base64Url(payload),
  svid_private_key
) → envelope_signature

The SVID private key proves this request came from the attested workload.

steps.label: 3. Embed DPoP Public Key
steps.value: The dpop_public_key field in the envelope locks the eventual token to this specific key pair. The PDP forwards the key thumbprint (JKT) to the CDP, which will embed it in the cnf claim of the issued token.

steps.explanation: The agent submits a Task Request Envelope to the PDP. This signed envelope contains: (1) the agent's SPIFFE ID derived from its SVID, (2) the requested scopes for the target resource, and (3) critically, the agent's ephemeral DPoP public key. By signing with the SVID private key, the agent proves it is the attested workload. The embedded DPoP public key will be used to bind the eventual token.
annotations: The Task Request Envelope is a CB4A-defined structure — it packages who is asking, what they need, and which key should be bound to the resulting credential.
The DPoP public key thumbprint (JKT) is computed per RFC 7638 and will appear in the cnf.jkt claim of the issued token.
The PDP evaluates policy (RBAC, OPA, etc.) against the agent's identity and requested scopes — it never generates or stores credentials.
The 60-second envelope TTL prevents replay attacks; the PDP must reject expired envelopes.

steps.label: POST /approve

artifacts.signing_key_hint: PDP Service Private Key (RSA-2048, kid: pdp-key-1)

steps.label: 2. Sign Decision with PDP Private Key
steps.value:
RSA-SHA256(
  canonicalized_decision_json,
  pdp_private_key
) → pdp_approval_sig

The CDP MUST verify this signature before minting any credential. An unsigned or invalidly-signed decision is rejected.

steps.label: 3. Key Separation Principle
steps.value:
The PDP signs a policy decision — it does NOT generate tokens or access credentials.
The CDP mints tokens — it does NOT evaluate policy.

This dual-control design means compromise of either component alone cannot produce an unauthorized credential.

steps.explanation: The PDP sends its signed policy approval to the CDP. The CDP will only mint a credential if the PDP's signature is valid. This step embodies CB4A's core security property: separating policy decisions (PDP) from credential issuance (CDP). The dpop_jkt field carries the agent's DPoP key thumbprint, which the CDP will embed in the token's cnf claim.
annotations: The CDP verifies the PDP's signature using a pre-shared or PKI-distributed public key — it never blindly trusts unsigned policy decisions.
The dpop_jkt (DPoP Key Thumbprint, RFC 7638) is the SHA-256 thumbprint of the agent's DPoP public key. The CDP embeds this in the token's cnf.jkt claim.
The 60-second approval TTL (iat→exp) prevents the CDP from minting tokens against stale or replayed policy decisions.
This separation is analogous to the two-person integrity control in nuclear launch systems — neither party can act alone.

steps.label: 200 OK — DPoP Token Issued

artifacts.signing_key_hint: CDP Token Signing Key (ES256, kid: cdp-token-key-1)

steps.label: 1. Verify PDP Approval Signature
steps.value: CDP first verifies the RSA-SHA256 signature on the policy decision received from the PDP. If invalid or expired, the CDP rejects the request without minting any token.

steps.label: 3. DPoP Key Binding (cnf.jkt)
steps.value:
The cnf.jkt claim is the SHA-256 JWK Thumbprint (RFC 7638) of the agent's DPoP public key.

This binding means: even if an attacker steals this token, they cannot use it — they would also need the DPoP private key to generate valid DPoP proofs.

steps.explanation: The CDP mints a short-lived (5-minute) DPoP-bound access token and delivers it to the agent (via the PDP response channel). The critical security feature is the cnf.jkt claim — a SHA-256 thumbprint of the agent's ephemeral DPoP public key. This cryptographically binds the token to that specific key pair: a stolen token is useless without the matching private key.
annotations: cnf.jkt = SHA-256(JWK_Thumbprint(dpop_public_key)) per RFC 7638. The Target API will verify this matches the DPoP proof's jwk header.
token_type: DPoP signals to the agent (and any OAuth 2.0-aware library) that DPoP proofs are required on every request.
The 300-second (5-minute) TTL limits the blast radius of any token compromise — far shorter than typical OAuth 2.0 bearer token lifetimes of 1 hour.
The agent never stored a long-lived API key — this token was minted on-demand, scoped to a single resource and task, and will expire in minutes.

steps.label: GET /api/resource
1. Build DPoP Proof Header (with JWK)
2. Build DPoP Proof Payload (URL-bound)
steps.value:
{
  "jti": "CB4A-proof-001",
  "htm": "GET",
  "htu": "https://api.example.com/resource",
  "iat": 1700000030
}

htm + htu bind this proof to exactly this HTTP method and URL.
This proof CANNOT be replayed against any other endpoint.

steps.label: 3. Sign with DPoP Private Key
steps.value:
ES256(
  Base64Url(header) + "." + Base64Url(payload),
  dpop_private_key
) → dpop_proof_sig

The private key never leaves the agent process. The signature proves the agent possesses it.

steps.label: 4. Target API Verification Steps
steps.value:
1. Verify DPoP proof signature using jwk from proof header.
2. Compute SHA-256 JWK thumbprint of proof's public key.
3. Compare thumbprint to access_token.cnf.jkt — must match.
4. Verify htm == 'GET' and htu == 'https://api.example.com/resource'.
5. Check iat is recent (within DPoP replay window, e.g. 60s).
6. Verify access token signature using CDP's public key.
7. Check scope includes 'read:resource'.
8. Authorize request.

steps.explanation: The agent makes the actual API call, presenting both the DPoP-bound access token (in the Authorization header) and a freshly minted DPoP proof JWT (in the DPoP header). The DPoP proof is bound to this exact HTTP method (GET) and URL, preventing replay. The Target API performs a multi-step verification: it checks the DPoP proof signature, verifies the public key thumbprint matches the access token's cnf.jkt, validates scopes, and then serves the resource.
annotations: Each API call requires a fresh DPoP proof — the jti (JWT ID) must be unique and the iat must be recent. The server should maintain a short-lived replay cache of seen JTIs.
The htm (HTTP Method) and htu (HTTP URI) claims tightly scope the proof: it cannot be stolen and replayed against /api/other-resource or with a POST method.
The agent's DPoP private key is ephemeral — generated at task start, used only for this task, and discarded when the task completes. This limits the key's exposure window.

steps.label: 200 OK — Resource Response
steps.explanation: The Target API has verified all CB4A credentials — the DPoP proof signature, key binding (cnf.jkt), access token validity, and scope — and returns the protected resource. The full CB4A Model B flow is complete: the agent obtained a scoped, short-lived, key-bound token without ever holding a long-lived API key. When the 5-minute token expires, the agent must repeat the full CB4A flow to get a new one.
annotations: At no point did the agent hold a long-lived API key. The token expires in 5 minutes and is scoped to a single resource.
If the agent is compromised after the token expires, the attacker gains nothing — they cannot reuse the expired token and cannot forge new ones without compromising the PDP+CDP pair.
The Cache-Control: no-store header prevents the response from being cached by any intermediary, which is standard practice for responses to authenticated API calls.
CB4A's key insight: 'credential sprawl' is eliminated by making credentials cheap, short-lived, and always brokered — never stored in agent configuration.
